**Chickpeas**, also called **garbanzo beans**, **bengal gram**, or **channa**,*[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)* are pulses used in the cuisines of South Asia, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

All chickpeas are overall rounded in shape, with a cleft at one end and a small point on the other. The two main types of chickpea are kabuli and desi. Kabuli are large, off-white chickpeas, while desi are smaller, darker, and more yellow.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Desi chickpeas may also be split and skinned to make **channa dal**.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) When cooked, chickpeas are firmer than other pulses and have a mild flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

Both main varieties of chickpea are ground to a flour, and the desi variety is referred to as **besan**.

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

While fresh chickpeas can be eaten, the majority are available dried or canned.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) As such, they are very shelf-stable at room temperature and easy to have on hand.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Both soaked and cooked chickpeas can be drained and frozen for several months.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) If the specific variety of chickpea or flour is not specified in your recipe, try to get the specific variety common to the cuisine of the recipe.

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Dried chickpeas must be soaked and cooked before use. The easiest way to do this is by covering them in cold water and letting them rest overnight.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) After soaking, whole chickpeas can be peeled by pinching and rolling between finger and thumb.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Cooking can be done at a regular boil or using a pressure cooker.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Kabuli chickpeas are more common in Mediterranean and West Asian cuisines, while desi chickpeas are more common in South Asia. They are the major ingredient in hummus and falafel,[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2) and they can also be included in stews, salads, curries, and more. Chickpea flour is used to make various fritters and flatbreads.[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

A variety of other pulses can be reasonably substituted for chickpeas, such as fava beans or cannellini beans.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)
